QF.id: XIII Trading, Clearing, Settlement and Risk Management_31

question.text: What is the key difference between a Trading Member-Clearing Member (TM-CM) and a Professional Clearing Member (PCM) in the derivatives clearing hierarchy?

answer.text: A TM-CM can both trade and clear, while a PCM only clears trades (for other TMs) and cannot trade on its own account
A TM-CM handles only proprietary trades, while a PCM handles only client trades
A TM-CM must be a bank, while a PCM can be any SEBI-registered entity
A PCM has higher position limits than a TM-CM because it clears for multiple members

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In the clearing hierarchy: Trading Member (TM) only trades; Trading Member-Clearing Member (TM-CM) both trades and clears its own trades; Professional Clearing Member (PCM) only clears trades executed by other TMs but does not trade itself; Self-Clearing Member (SCM) clears only its own trades. **The Trap:** PCMs do NOT trade — they provide clearing services to TMs who lack clearing membership. A TM-CM is not restricted to proprietary trades; it handles both proprietary and client trades. **Cross-Exam Context:** This TM/CM hierarchy is identical across currency (Series I), interest rate (Series IV), and equity (Series VIII) derivative segments — a core Series XIII cross-segment concept. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Banks and custodians often become PCMs as it aligns with their settlement/custody expertise without needing trading infrastructure. The separation enhances systemic risk management.
